⚡️ The first Agent Execution Governance (AEG) platform

Secure Every AI Agent Action Before It Executes

Sentry and Datadog tell you after an AI agent has corrupted your database or leaked credentials. Mergen is the inline Execution and Security Gateway that physically blocks hazardous agent actions before they reach your runtime, databases, or cloud infrastructure.

Request Early AccessJoin Design Partner Program
npx mergen-server
✓ Deterministic local policy gate✓ Human-in-the-loop (HITL) approval✓ Every blocked action logged & hash-chained✓ Override corpus: your infrastructure DNA✓ Agent safety CI gate✓ All data on your infrastructure
< 1msGate evaluation latencydeterministic, zero network I/O
94%Threat block rate33-threat validation harness
99.9%Gate reliabilitystrict deterministic policy match
100%Local executionno cloud credentials exposed
mergen — autonomous incident loop

Watch: 60s Quick Start

01 // The Difference

The gate runs before
the handler does.

Scenario A — Solo developer, no code reviewer

Without Mergen

0m
Write the change
Touch the same file that caused last month's outage.
0m
Run tests
Tests pass. No reviewer. Ship it.
4h
Production alert fires
Same failure mode. No one warned you.
4h+
Reconstruct context
Grep logs. Trace the history. Piece it together under pressure.

With Mergen

0m
Stage the change
git add auth_middleware.ts
0s
Guard runs
Cross-references staged files against incident history.
1s
Warning surfaced
"This file was in Incident #388 — do not increase stack depth > 4."
2m
Fix before shipping
Adjust the change. The outage never happens.
✅

Result: The bug never ships. Incident history is your reviewer — working silently at commit time.

Scenario B — Solo developer, visual audit trail of agent activity

Without Mergen

0m
Agent task started
custom-agent --task "refactor users schema"
5m
Wall of stdio text
Agent prints thousands of lines of terminal logs.
15m
System breaks
Local server crashes. You have no idea what mutated.
30m
Trace file edits
Manually inspect git diffs and trace agent tool logs.
1h
Find root cause
Piece together that the agent deleted DB config on step 84.

With Mergen

0m
Agent task started
custom-agent --task "refactor users schema"
1s
Gateway inline tracing
Gateway intercepts and indexes every tool call.
5s
Compile audit log
Hash-chains every command, read, and write.
10s
Generate living map
Renders visual map of exactly what the agent modified.
30s
Spot mutation
Instantly isolate that step 84 modified DB config.
✅

Result: Living map generated in real-time. You immediately see the exact files, environment variables, and system commands changed by the agent.

Scenario C — AI agent attempts a destructive command

Without Mergen

0ms
Agent calls execute_fix
{ command: "terraform destroy prod" }
0ms
Handler runs immediately
No gate. No check. No approval.
1s
terraform destroy executes
Production infrastructure torn down.
∞
Incident declared
Human wakes up to a destroyed environment.

With Mergen

0ms
Agent calls execute_fix
{ command: "terraform destroy prod" }
<1ms
Local gate evaluates
Pattern "destroy" matched → rule block_destructive_commands
<1ms
Handler never runs
MCP error returned before execution.
<1ms
Blunder logged
Recorded to agent-blunders.json with hash chain.
🚫 Tool call blocked by Mergen local policy gate.
Tool: execute_fix
Reason: Destructive command pattern matched.
See: GET /agent-blunders
✅

Result: Production never touched. The gate runs before the handler — deterministically, in under 1ms, with no LLM involved.

Scenario D — Human overrides compound into persistent agent policy

Without Mergen

0m
Constraint found
Write warning in README: "Never let AI rewrite auth_middleware.ts."
2wk
Rule is forgotten
README changes or is omitted from agent context window.
3mo
Engineer leaves
The context leaves with them. New agent has no idea.
3mo
Agent rewrites file
Agent replaces authentication logic with a broken pattern.

With Mergen

0m
Policy registered
Register override: "Block automated commits to auth_middleware.ts."
1s
Hook encoded
Local gate registers the constraint automatically.
3mo
Engineer leaves
Constraint remains in SQLite override corpus, queryable & active.
3mo
Agent edit blocked
Agent tries to edit file. Git hook blocks commit instantly.
✅

Result: Overrides persist as machine-enforceable rules. The execution gate blocks agent violations even when you forget they exist.

03 // How It Works

Four steps from tool call
to target runtime.

STEP 1

Define local security policies

Set up rules matching command patterns, file paths, and environment settings. Rules can block outright or require validation.

STEP 2

Intercept agent tool calls

MCP tool calls, terminal commands, and filesystem modifications pass through the local gate before execution.

STEP 3

Enforce deterministic rules

Mergen checks commands against safety rules and SQLite history in <1ms without LLM latency, blocking destructive actions.

STEP 4

HITL approval & audit logs

Pauses execution for unverified actions to request human approval. Logs every block to ~/.mergen/agent-blunders.json.

CLAUDE CODE

MCP Tool Ingestion

Synchronous stdio stream

CURSOR

IDE Execution Wrapper

Intercepts terminal runs

CI/CD PIPELINE

Git Hooks & Actions

Autonomous PR evaluation

CUSTOM AGENTS

SDK / API Integrations

Zero-trust runtime gate

›
SECURITY GATEWAY

Mergen

Execution & Security Gateway
—SQLite override corpus
—Local policy engine
—Deterministic gates
—Agent Blunder Log
—PII & Secret Shield
›
TARGET

OS & File System

Enforced sandboxed paths
TARGET

Cloud Infrastructure

AWS, GCP, Terraform APIs
TARGET

Databases & VPCs

Isolated query execution
COMMS

Slack HITL

Operator approval webhook
05 // Evaluation

Evaluated before
you ever use it.

We built an agent security regression eval harness. Every pull request that updates local gateway rules must pass this validation suite — 33 real agent threat scenarios and 10 distinct security failure categories. When we say 94% threat block rate, that number is reproducible and falsifiable.

Overall Block Rate
94%
31 of 33 agent threats blocked instantly
Test payload size33 threats
Blocked31 / 33
False positives2 (advisory)
Last eval runJun 16, 2026
Failure classFixturesAccuracy
Jailbreak / Prompt Injection
5/5
100%
Destructive Shell Commands
5/5
100%
VPC / Network Egress Violations
3/3
100%
Credential / Secret Extraction
3/3
100%
Recursive Tool Execution Loops
3/3
100%
Host Directory Traversal
3/3
100%
Database Schema Drop Attempt
2/2
100%
Shadow Package Installation
3/3
100%
Unapproved Environment Mutation
3/3
100%
API Token Theft Attempt
1/1
100%
False positive (valid commands)
Advisory warnings only — doesn't block developer flow
0/2
0%

Why this matters: Most post-incident monitoring tools are evaluated by the engineers who built them, on the incidents they chose. Mergen ships a public eval harness — the same suite that gates every release. The 2 failures are documented: the detector fires on liveness probe and Prometheus scrape errors when it shouldn't. Fix is in the roadmap; hiding it is not.

View Full JSON Baseline →
02 // The Problem

AI agents can execute anything.
Nothing enforces what they’re allowed to.

Autonomous agents inherit full tool access with zero authorization checks. System prompts are probabilistic — they get ignored under pressure and adversarial injection. RAG policies in vector databases are advisory suggestions. None of them physically prevent a destructive command from reaching your infrastructure. Mergen does.

01

Agents inherit full tool access with zero authorization

Every MCP tool call is a potential production mutation.

AI coding agents inherit the permissions of the engineer who started the server — which means they can call any tool, execute any command, and mutate any system state with zero scope, role check, or approval flow. An agent asked to "clean up old records" has the same tool access as one explicitly told to destroy infrastructure.

02

Prompts are advisory — they are not enforcement

System prompts are suggestions. LLMs ignore them under pressure.

AI agents trained to be helpful will follow a system prompt instruction under normal conditions. Under adversarial prompt injection, jailbreak, or an unexpected context shift, they will not. The only reliable enforcement is a deterministic layer outside the LLM's reasoning path — one that physically intercepts the action before the handler runs.

03

Monitoring is reactive — the breach already happened

Datadog fires after the agent destroyed the environment.

PagerDuty pages a human. Datadog shows what the agent did. By then the schema is migrated, the infrastructure is torn down, or the credentials are in the logs. An alert after the fact is not governance. Mergen is the inline gate that intercepts the tool call before any of that happens.

04

CI pipelines have no agent governance layer

AI-generated PRs and deployments bypass human review at scale

As agents generate pull requests and trigger deployments autonomously, your CI pipeline becomes a production mutation surface with no mandatory human checkpoint. Mergen’s CI gate enforces deterministic blast-radius analysis and HITL approval before any autonomous change merges — the governance layer your pipeline is missing.

The missing layer is not more observability. It is a deterministic execution gate — one that physically intercepts agent actions before they reach your OS, terminal, or cloud provider, and enforces the policy your team has earned through every incident.

Talk to us
04 // Core Systems

Control that enforces.
Safety that compounds.

🔒
01

Local Execution Gate — Deterministic, Not Advisory

Every MCP tool call passes through a synchronous local policy engine before the handler runs. Pattern matched against your JSON rules in under 1ms — no LLM, no network, no probabilistic guardrails. PASS calls the handler. BLOCK returns a structured error with a specific guided alternative — the agent reformulates and retries within policy instead of stopping dead. The AI IDE waits for the response; the gate decides before any code executes.

# agent calls execute_fix with "terraform destroy prod"
→ tool-guard: evaluating in <1ms
→ pattern matched: "terraform destroy" → rule block_destructive_commands
→ handler: BLOCKED (never invoked)
→ blunder logged: agent-blunders.json
🚫 Mergen policy gate blocked this tool call.
Why: Local Gate: Destructive command pattern matched.
What to do instead: Run `terraform plan -destroy` to preview
  the blast radius, then request human approval before proceeding.
# agent reformulates → calls analyze_runtime → requests HITL approval
✅ Handler runs within policy.

🧑‍💻
02

Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) — Held Promise Approval

For flagged-but-not-blocked calls (schema migrations, high blast-radius commands), the gate holds the Promise until a human approves or denies. An outbound webhook fires to Slack or any HTTP endpoint with approve/deny URLs. The AI IDE blocks — MCP stdio is naturally async, so it waits indefinitely for the JSON-RPC response. No polling. No re-submission. One click resolves the hold.

# agent calls execute_fix with "prisma migrate deploy"
→ pattern matched: "prisma migrate" → rule hold_schema_mutations
→ HOLD: Promise suspended, token issued
→ webhook fired → MERGEN_HITL_WEBHOOK_URL
# operator clicks approve in Slack...
→ POST /hitl/approve?token=a3f9… → Promise resolved
✅ Tool call approved. Handler now executing.

🧬
03

Override Corpus — Infrastructure DNA

Every human override becomes machine-readable policy. After six months: your Friday settlement windows, compliance holds, and on-call preferences form your specific operational DNA — enforcing invariants before any autonomous action triggers. The algorithm is reproducible. This corpus is not.

🛡️
04

Agent Blunder Log — CISO Insurance

Every blocked action is hash-chained to a tamper-evident log: allowlist blocks, corpus halts, planning gates, policy intercepts. The total prevented count is the board-deck answer to "why would you trust an AI agent with production?" Wired automatically — no setup required.

⚙️
05

Agent Policy Calibration

Mergen calibrates policy rules dynamically to your specific codebase and infrastructure context. As your team adjusts and tags action overrides, the local gateway updates its rule definitions. Every action block is logged instantly.

MRG
Mergen APP 3:17 PM
🚫 Agent Tool Call Intercepted (Blocked)
Security Intercept Summary
• Actor: cursor-agent-server
• Action: rm -rf /var/log/nginx/*
• Rule Matched: block_destructive_commands
• Result: Synchronously blocked in <1ms; blunder logged.

📊
06

Measurable Developer ROI

The local gateway tracks blocked security exposures, unapproved database mutations, and destructive commands. It generates a weekly ROI report showing how many hours were saved by preventing accidental database wipes, deleted configurations, or security leaks.

👤
07

Shadow Mode — 30-Day Audit Track Record

Before enforcing strict command blocks, run Mergen in shadow mode. It observes and records what actions it would have blocked or suspended, giving your team a full trust report before you activate strict enforcement gates.

🚨
08

Pre-commit Incident Guard

Before you ship, Mergen cross-references every staged file against your incident history. “This file was in 3 incidents last month” — the question a code reviewer would ask, encoded as a git hook. The corpus working before the incident happens.

auth_middleware.ts — Mergen Contextmcp.json
// Mergen: Historical Context found
⚠️
This file was modified in Incident #388 (OOM Kill).
Reason: Recursive token validation on nested JWTs.
Constraint: Do not increase stack depth > 4.
export const validateToken = (token: string) => {
  // checking depth...

the enforcement gate.

06 // Getting Started

Gate running in 60 seconds.
Pilot success: first blocked action logged.

01
zero config · Node.js 18+

Start the local gate

Run one command. Mergen starts the local policy gate and binds to 127.0.0.1:3000. Every MCP tool call your AI agent makes now passes through the gate before execution.

npx mergen-server

Verify the gate is live: curl http://127.0.0.1:3000/health → { "ok": true, "gate": "active" }

02
JSON rules · local config

Define Safety Policies

Create a local policy file. Define matching patterns for commands that must be blocked outright, and commands that require human-in-the-loop (HITL) approval.

{
  "block_rules": [
    { "pattern": "terraform destroy", "action": "BLOCK" },
    { "pattern": "rm -rf", "action": "BLOCK" }
  ],
  "hitl_rules": [
    { "pattern": "prisma migrate", "action": "HOLD" }
  ]
}

Safety policies are loaded locally and evaluated deterministically in <1ms.

03
Claude Code · Cursor · VS Code

Add to Your AI IDE

Register Mergen as an MCP gateway server in your AI editor. The gateway wraps stdio transport, intercepting all tool calls before they run.

# Add to Claude Code configuration
claude mcp add mergen --transport stdio -- npx mergen-server

# Or register as an execution gate wrapper in Cursor/VS Code

Ask your agent: "delete the logs folder" — the local gateway intercepts and blocks it.

04
shadow mode · corpus enforcement

Build the Override Corpus

Run in shadow mode to analyze agent requests without active blocking, building your repository's specific operational DNA. Switch to active enforcement once verified.

# Run in shadow mode (audit actions, no blocking)
MERGEN_SHADOW_MODE=true npx mergen-server

# Retrieve blocked actions from the blunder log
curl http://127.0.0.1:3000/agent-blunders

Blocked actions are recorded in the blunder log at ~/.mergen/agent-blunders.json with a secure hash chain.
